scenario: Beacon Insurance configures a Camunda 8.8 claim-validation service task in a process that handles 4200 claims per day. The developer must understand exactly into which scope the engine merges variables on a complete-job command versus a fail-job command so the downstream gateway and the next BPMN activity receive the validation outcome variable as expected.
question: How does the Camunda 8.8 engine merge variables supplied with a complete-job command versus a fail-job command from a worker?

options.text: complete-job stores its variables only in the task's local scope and discards them at the next gateway, while fail-job propagates its variables up to the process instance scope so the next worker can read them later

complete-job propagates its variables up to the process instance scope as JSON, while fail-job rejects any payload that contains variables and only accepts the remaining-retries count from the failing worker

complete-job and fail-job both propagate their variables up from the task scope to the process instance scope; the only behavioural difference is whether remaining retries are decremented on the broker side

complete-job propagates its variables from the task scope to higher scopes of the process instance, while fail-job only creates variables in the local scope of the failing task during the failure event

a.text: Incorrect. The scope semantics are reversed: complete-job propagates variables from the task scope to higher scopes, while fail-job keeps the supplied variables local to the failing task.

b.text: Incorrect. fail-job can carry variables; the docs note that failing with variables is useful for example to add detail to an incident or to record partial progress for the next attempt.

c.text: Incorrect. complete-job and fail-job differ in their variable scope semantics: complete propagates upward, fail stays in the local task scope only.

d.text: Correct. The Completing-or-failing-jobs section states that on complete-job variables are propagated from the task scope to higher scopes of the process instance, while on fail-job the variables are only created in the local scope of the failing task.

explanation: Variables attached to a complete-job command are merged from the job's task scope up into higher scopes of the process instance so that downstream activities can read them. Variables attached to a fail-job command are created only in the local scope of the failing task, which is useful when failing with extra detail for an incident or for partial progress state.

docs.title: Completing or failing jobs with variables
